name: Spain
summary: Spain combines Atlantic, continental and Mediterranean influences depending on region, altitude and distance from the sea.
examTips: Study Spain by comparing Atlantic, inland continental and Mediterranean regions.
Altitude is a major theme in Spain because it helps preserve acidity and freshness.
Oak ageing is especially important in classic regions such as Rioja.

rias-baixas.name: Rias Baixas
rias-baixas.summary: Cool Atlantic region known for fresh, high-acid aromatic whites with saline freshness.
rias-baixas.styleSummary: Fresh high-acid whites with citrus, stone fruit and saline notes.
examTips: Think Atlantic, high rainfall and fresh whites.
High acidity and saline notes are useful tasting clues.

Albarino.summary: Aromatic high-acid white grape with citrus, peach and saline character, strongly linked to cool maritime conditions.
Albarino.style: High-acid aromatic white with citrus, peach and saline notes.
viticulture: Needs good canopy management in humid conditions
examTips: High acidity plus maritime freshness is a strong clue.
Usually associated with fresh unoaked styles.

rueda.name: Rueda
rueda.summary: Inland elevated region known for fresh aromatic whites, especially Verdejo.
rueda.styleSummary: Fresh aromatic whites, especially from Verdejo.
examTips: Altitude and cool nights help preserve freshness despite warm days.
Verdejo is the key grape to remember.

Verdejo.summary: Fresh aromatic white grape giving citrus, herb and stone-fruit character with good acidity.
Verdejo.style: Citrus, herb and stone-fruit white with good freshness.
viticulture: Performs well with warm days and cool nights
examTips: Fresh aromatic whites from inland Spain often point to Verdejo.
Cool nights are important for retaining acidity.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: Aromatic white grape producing fresh citrus and herbaceous styles in cooler elevated sites.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Fresh citrus and herbaceous white with pronounced aromatics.
viticulture: Can retain freshness in cooler elevated sites
examTips: Expect higher acidity and more obvious herbaceous notes.
Usually made in a fresh cool-fermented style.

rioja.name: Rioja
rioja.summary: Classic Spanish region known for oak-aged reds, subregional variation and ageing classifications.
rioja.styleSummary: Can range from fresher elegant styles to riper fuller expressions depending on subregion and oak ageing.
examTips: Ageing classifications and oak use are key Rioja themes.
Tempranillo is the core red grape to remember.

Tempranillo.summary: Spain's flagship red grape, giving red and black fruit with spice and savoury development in oak-aged wines.
Tempranillo.style: Red and black fruit, medium to high body, spice and leather with age.
viticulture: Can retain freshness in suitable sites
examTips: Oak-aged Tempranillo is central to classic Rioja.
Leather and spice often appear with bottle age.

Garnacha.summary: Warm-climate red grape bringing ripe fruit, spice and body to Rioja blends.
Garnacha.style: Riper red fruit and spice, fuller body.
examTips: Adds ripeness and body in blends.
Usually softer and riper than Tempranillo.

Graciano.summary: Perfumed dark-fruited grape used to add freshness, acidity and structure to blends.
Graciano.style: Perfumed dark fruit and freshness, often used to add acidity and structure.
viticulture: Lower yielding and harder to ripen
examTips: High acidity makes it useful in blends.
Usually not the dominant grape.

Mazuelo.summary: Late-ripening grape used mainly for colour, tannin and acidity in blends.
Mazuelo.style: Adds colour, tannin and acidity to blends.
examTips: Think structure rather than dominant flavour role.
Usually appears as a supporting blending grape.

Viura.summary: Traditional white grape of Rioja, able to make fresh or oak-aged styles.
Viura.style: Citrus, floral notes and can be fresh or oak-aged.
examTips: Can appear in more traditional oak-influenced white Rioja.
More neutral than very aromatic white grapes.

ribera-del-duero.name: Ribera del Duero
ribera-del-duero.summary: High-altitude inland red wine region known for concentrated yet fresh Tempranillo.
ribera-del-duero.styleSummary: Structured reds with ripe fruit, firm tannin and freshness from altitude.
examTips: Usually more powerful and concentrated than Rioja.
Altitude and diurnal range are key balancing factors.

Tempranillo.summary: Concentrated, structured Tempranillo showing ripe black fruit, firm tannin and freshness.
Tempranillo.style: Ripe black fruit, firm tannin, fuller body and fresh acidity.
viticulture: Warm days and cool nights help ripening balance
examTips: Think fuller body and firmer tannin than classic Rioja.
Altitude helps preserve acidity.

priorat.name: Priorat
priorat.summary: Warm dry Mediterranean region making concentrated reds from old vines on poor slate soils.
priorat.styleSummary: Powerful concentrated reds from warm dry conditions and poor slate soils.
examTips: Slate soils and low yields are key clues.
Expect concentration, body and high alcohol.

Garnacha.summary: Old-vine Garnacha gives ripe fruit, spice, concentration and high alcohol in Priorat.
Garnacha.style: Ripe black and red fruit, spice, high alcohol and concentration.
viticulture: Very low yields in poor soils
examTips: Warmth plus poor soils drive concentration.
Old vines are commonly associated with quality.

Carinena.summary: Structured blending grape contributing dark fruit, acidity and mineral edge.
Carinena.style: Dark fruit, high acidity, structure and mineral edge.
viticulture: Thrives in warm dry sites with low yields
examTips: Higher acidity and structure than Garnacha.
Often supports blends rather than standing alone.

navarra.name: Navarra
navarra.summary: Diverse region producing rosé, reds and some whites across mixed climate zones.
navarra.styleSummary: A diverse region producing fresh rosé, reds and some whites.
examTips: Climate varies from cooler north to warmer south.
Garnacha is especially important for rosé and reds.

Garnacha.summary: Important warm-climate grape used for ripe-fruited reds and fresh rosé.
Garnacha.style: Ripe red fruit and spice, widely used for rosé and reds.
examTips: Remember its importance in Navarra rosé.
Usually riper and fuller than cooler-climate red varieties.

Tempranillo.summary: Versatile Spanish red grape giving fresh red and black fruit in medium-bodied styles.
Tempranillo.style: Fresh red and black fruit with medium body.
examTips: Usually less oak-focused than classic Rioja examples.
Balanced medium-bodied style is common.

catalunya.name: Catalunya
catalunya.summary: Large varied Mediterranean region producing a wide range of still wines.
catalunya.styleSummary: Wide range of still wines from an extensive and varied region.
examTips: Think diversity rather than one single signature still wine.
Macabeo is useful to remember because it appears in both still and sparkling wines.

Garnacha.summary: Warm-climate grape giving ripe fruit, spice and body.
Garnacha.style: Ripe red fruit, spice and medium to full body.
examTips: Warm Mediterranean conditions favour ripe styles.

Macabeo.summary: Neutral to lightly floral white grape used in fresh still wines and also in sparkling production.
Macabeo.style: Neutral to lightly floral white with citrus and orchard fruit.
viticulture: Useful in both still and sparkling wine
examTips: More neutral than highly aromatic varieties.
Useful to remember in both still and sparkling contexts.

la-mancha.name: La Mancha
la-mancha.summary: Large hot dry inland region known for volume production and improving quality.
la-mancha.styleSummary: Large hot dry inland region producing volume and increasingly better quality wines.
examTips: Large-scale production is an important theme.
Altitude helps moderate the otherwise hot inland climate.

Tempranillo.summary: Ripe-fruited inland Tempranillo with moderate acidity and fruit-driven style.
Tempranillo.style: Ripe dark fruit with moderate acidity.
examTips: Hot inland climate usually means riper fruit profile.

Airen.summary: Very drought-tolerant white grape often used for simple, light wines.
Airen.style: Neutral white, generally simple and light.
examTips: Important for understanding Spain's volume wine production.

jumilla.name: Jumilla
jumilla.summary: Hot dry region famous for powerful Monastrell-based reds.
jumilla.styleSummary: Hot dry region known for ripe, powerful Monastrell.
examTips: Think hot, dry and powerful.
Monastrell is the key grape here.

Monastrell.summary: Hot-climate red grape giving dark fruit, spice, high alcohol and firm tannin.
Monastrell.style: Dark fruit, spice, high alcohol and firm tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in hot dry climates
examTips: Heat and drought tolerance are key clues for Monastrell.

valencia.name: Valencia
valencia.summary: Warm Mediterranean region producing ripe reds and a range of wine styles.
valencia.styleSummary: Warm-climate region producing both reds and whites in a range of styles.
examTips: Think warm Mediterranean ripeness with some coastal moderation.

Monastrell.summary: Ripe dark-fruited warm-climate red grape with full body and spice.
Monastrell.style: Ripe dark fruit and spice with full body.
examTips: Often associated with warm Mediterranean climates.

penedes.name: Penedes
penedes.summary: Catalan region strongly associated with Cava, where altitude helps preserve freshness despite Mediterranean warmth.
penedes.styleSummary: Traditional method sparkling wines with fresh citrus fruit, moderate body and some autolytic complexity.
examTips: Cava is made by the traditional method, not tank method.
Altitude is an important moderating influence in warm Mediterranean Spain.
Know the classic Macabeo, Xarel-lo and Parellada blend.

Macabeo.summary: Widely used Cava grape bringing apple, citrus and freshness.
Macabeo.style: Apple, citrus and fresh moderate body.
viticulture: Useful in warm climates when freshness can be retained
examTips: Often gives freshness and fruit rather than structure.
Alias Viura appears in still Spain too.

Xarel-lo.summary: Important Cava grape contributing body, structure and ageing potential.
Xarel-lo.style: More body and structure than the lightest components in Cava.
viticulture: Performs well in warm Mediterranean climate
examTips: Often the structural backbone of the blend.
Useful to contrast with lighter Parellada.

Parellada.summary: Higher-altitude grape adding finesse, floral notes and acidity to Cava blends.
examTips: High altitude helps preserve acidity.
Usually the lightest of the classic trio.

cava.name: Cava
cava.style: Traditional method sparkling wine with citrus, apple and moderate autolytic complexity.
cava.keyPoint: Traditional method sparkling wine from Spain, often based in Penedes.

cava-reserva-gran-reserva.name: Cava Reserva / Gran Reserva
cava-reserva-gran-reserva.style: More autolytic, complex sparkling wine with greater ageing influence.
cava-reserva-gran-reserva.aging: Longer lees ageing than basic Cava
cava-reserva-gran-reserva.keyPoint: Longer ageing increases toast and complexity.

jerez.name: Jerez
jerez.summary: Warm southern Spanish region producing Sherry, with styles shaped by fortification, flor and oxidative ageing.
jerez.styleSummary: Fortified wines ranging from very dry flor-aged styles to rich oxidatively aged and sweet wines.
examTips: Know the difference between biological ageing under flor and oxidative ageing.
The solera system is central to style consistency in Sherry.
Fino/Manzanilla are the lightest dry styles; Oloroso is richer and fully oxidative.

Palomino.summary: Neutral white grape forming the base of most dry Sherry styles.
Palomino.style: Neutral base wine that gains character mainly from ageing process.
viticulture: Well suited to chalky albariza soils
winemaking: Fortified then aged under flor or oxidatively
examTips: Palomino is neutral, so ageing method matters most.
Fino and Oloroso start from different decisions about flor and fortification.

Pedro Ximenez.summary: White grape used for intensely sweet raisined wines and sometimes blending.
Pedro Ximenez.style: Very sweet, raisined, figgy and syrupy.
examTips: Sun drying before fermentation is a key process point.
PX is one of the richest sweet fortified styles.

Moscatel.summary: Aromatic grape used for sweet fortified wines with floral and grapey notes.
Moscatel.style: Sweet, floral, grapey and orange-peel character.
examTips: Moscatel is more obviously aromatic than PX.
Used for sweet Sherry styles rather than the driest styles.

fino.name: Fino
fino.style: Very dry, pale, light-bodied Sherry with almond and bread notes.
fino.keyPoint: Flor protects the wine from oxidation.

manzanilla.name: Manzanilla
manzanilla.style: Very dry, delicate and especially saline Sherry from Sanlucar de Barrameda.
manzanilla.keyPoint: A fino style linked specifically to the coastal town of Sanlucar.

amontillado.name: Amontillado
amontillado.style: Dry, amber Sherry combining flor-derived savouriness with later oxidative nuttiness.
amontillado.aging: Biological ageing followed by oxidative ageing
amontillado.keyPoint: Starts under flor, then continues oxidatively.

palo-cortado.name: Palo Cortado
palo-cortado.style: Rarer style combining aromatic finesse with richer oxidative texture.
palo-cortado.aging: Oxidative ageing after atypical flor development
palo-cortado.keyPoint: Bridges some traits of Amontillado and Oloroso.

oloroso.name: Oloroso
oloroso.style: Dry to medium style with walnut, spice and fuller body from oxidative ageing.
oloroso.keyPoint: No flor protection, so style is richer and darker.

cream-sherry.name: Cream Sherry
cream-sherry.style: Sweetened richer Sherry with nutty, caramel and dried-fruit character.
cream-sherry.aging: Oxidatively aged base wines then sweetened

px.name: Pedro Ximenez (PX)
px.style: Very sweet, dark, raisined fortified wine.
px.aging: Oxidative maturation after very sweet base wine production
px.keyPoint: One of the richest sweet fortified wine styles.
